planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring as new growth emerges, or plant container-grown stock in spring or early fall in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; Siebold's hosta is one of the largest and most imposing hosta species and grows slowly to a massive clump but rarely needs division.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; Siebold's hosta performs best with morning sun and afternoon shade, or light, dappled shade from deciduous trees. Hosta sieboldiana is the most imposing of all hosta species in size and foliage impact — producing enormous, deeply corrugated, puckered, blue-gray to blue-green leaves that can reach 12–15 inches long and 10–13 inches wide on mature plants, with a velvety, glaucous surface that gives the leaves a distinctive waxy sheen and outstanding slug resistance. The near-white, pale-lavender flowers bloom in June–July on stems just above the leaf mound and are relatively inconspicuous compared to the spectacular foliage. This is a slow-growing but very long-lived hosta that takes several years to achieve its full, majestic clump size — but once established it is essentially maintenance-free and can remain undisturbed for decades. H. sieboldiana is the parent of many of the most famous large-leaved hosta cultivars including 'Frances Williams,' 'Elegans,' and 'Great Expectations.'

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; Fortune's hosta is an adaptable, vigorous, medium-to-large hosta that tolerates a wider range of light conditions than many other hosta species.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; Fortune's hosta tolerates brief periods of morning sun better than most other hostas, making it adaptable to a wider range of garden positions. Hosta fortunei is a medium-to-large hosta species that was one of the first hostas popularized in Western gardens and has since given rise to an enormous range of named cultivars — particularly the classic variegated forms 'Albopicta' (green-margined yellow centers fading to all-green by midsummer), 'Aureomarginata' (yellow-margined green), and 'Albomarginata' (white-margined green). The species itself has smooth to slightly corrugated, gray-green to olive-green leaves 7–9 inches long, and lavender flowers in July–August. It is among the most widely planted and adaptable of the medium-large hostas and tolerates more sun and occasional drought stress than larger-leaved species, making it a reliable choice for beginner and experienced shade gardeners alike.

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; H. ventricosa is one of the more heat-tolerant hosta species, performing adequately through zone 9 where most large-leaved hostas struggle in summer heat.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; H. ventricosa grows well in deeper shade than many hostas and is one of the heat-tolerant species that performs more reliably in warm-climate zones 7–9. Hosta ventricosa is a large-growing species with very glossy, deep-green, broadly ovate to heart-shaped leaves 8–12 inches long with prominently impressed veins that give the leaves a quilted appearance; unlike most large hostas the leaves are glossy rather than glaucous (waxy). The striking deep-violet to violet-purple flowers are among the most colorful of any hosta species, blooming in July–August on tall scapes that stand well above the leaf mound — they are attractive both fresh and as dried stems. The cultivar 'Aureomaculata' has yellow-centered leaves in spring that turn all green by summer; 'Aureomarginated' has yellow-margined leaves. H. ventricosa produces viable seed that germinates readily — one of the few hosta species that breeds relatively true from seed.

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in rich, moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; August Moon develops its most vivid golden-yellow foliage color with 2–4 hours of morning sun — in deep shade the leaves remain more chartreuse-green.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; August Moon develops its characteristic golden-yellow color best with some morning sun — too much shade keeps it chartreuse, while too much sun scorches the leaf margins. Hosta 'August Moon' is one of the oldest and most reliable of the gold-leaved hostas, producing large (8–10 inch), rounded, slightly corrugated, yellow-green to golden-yellow leaves that brighten dramatically in late spring and maintain good color through summer. The near-white to pale-lavender flowers bloom in July–August on tall scapes above the foliage. August Moon was introduced around 1968 by Paul Aden and remains a benchmark gold hosta — vigorous, adaptable, and more sun-tolerant than most large hostas, making it easier to site in gardens that receive more light than ideal for blue-leaved types. The golden foliage creates outstanding contrast against blue hostas, dark-leaved heucheras, or green ferns in shade garden compositions.

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in rich, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; Striptease is a relatively fast-growing hosta that forms good-sized clumps within 3–4 years and is one of the most eye-catching of the tricolor hostas.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; Striptease retains good variegation in moderate to full shade, making it more versatile than many hostas that need some sun to hold their color. Hosta 'Striptease' is one of the most visually striking of all hosta cultivars — named for its unique tricolor variegation pattern in which dark-green leaf margins surround a bright gold-yellow center separated by a narrow, precise bright-white stripe that runs the full perimeter of the central gold section; this crisp, three-colored, sharply-defined pattern is unlike most hosta variegation and gives 'Striptease' a bold graphic quality rare in shade-garden plants. The medium-sized leaves are 7–8 inches long with an upright, slightly wavy habit. Near-white lavender flowers appear in late summer. 'Striptease' was introduced by Tony Avent and received the American Hosta Society's Alex J. Summers Distinguished Merit Hosta award. It is a vigorous, relatively fast-growing cultivar that forms impressive clumps within 3–4 seasons.
